items.termEn: Dark roast coffee always contains more caffeine than light roast coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Roasting changes the flavor and color of the bean, but most of the caffeine stays intact.
That is why a dark roast and a light roast from the same beans carry similar caffeine levels.
Light roast can match dark roast in caffeine.

items.termEn: Robusta contains on average more caffeine than arabica.
items.explanationShort:
Robusta has roughly twice the caffeine of arabica.
That difference comes from the plant species itself, not from how it is roasted or brewed.
Robusta or arabica decides, not the roast.

items.termEn: Decaffeinated coffee contains absolutely zero caffeine.
items.explanationShort:
Decaf removes most caffeine, but never all of it.
Because of that, a typical decaf still contains 5 to 15 mg of caffeine per cup.
Decaf still has a kick, just smaller.

items.termEn: An espresso has more caffeine than a regular cup of drip coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Espresso is highly concentrated but tiny, about 1 oz (30 ml) per shot.
Drip coffee is milder per sip but much larger, around 8 to 12 oz.
Stronger taste does not mean more caffeine.

items.termEn: Caffeine in a cup depends mostly on how long the coffee is brewed.
items.explanationShort:
Brew time plays a role, but species, dose, and cup size matter more.
That is why an espresso and a French press can carry similar caffeine.
Bean type and cup size beat brew time.

items.termEn: Caffeine sensitivity is the same for everyone.
items.explanationShort:
Caffeine hits everyone differently, mostly because of genetics.
In some people, genetics alone can make caffeine last twice as long in the body.
One person's limit is another's warm-up.

items.termEn: A coffee at 3 PM can still affect sleep at midnight.
items.explanationShort:
In most adults, half the caffeine is still in the body 5 hours after drinking.
After about 10 hours, most of it is finally gone.
The clock matters as much as the cup.

items.termEn: Robusta coffee is always lower quality than arabica.
items.explanationShort:
Arabica is smoother and more complex, while robusta is bolder and higher in caffeine.
That is why many blends add robusta, as it holds up well under steam in milk drinks.
Arabica-only is a style choice, not a quality guarantee.

items.termEn: A light roast has a weaker flavor than a dark roast.
items.explanationShort:
Light roasts taste fruity and bright.
Dark roasts taste smoky and bitter.
The roast changes taste, not strength.

items.termEn: Most healthy adults can safely drink up to 400 mg of caffeine per day.
items.explanationShort:
400 mg per day is the safety threshold established for most healthy adults.
Above that limit, jitters, headaches, or poor sleep become more likely.
That is roughly 5 to 6 espressos.

items.termEn: Cold brew and iced coffee use the same beans but a completely different brewing method.
items.explanationShort:
Cold brew steeps grounds in cold water for 12 to 24 hours.
Iced coffee is hot-brewed coffee poured over ice.
Same beans, two different drinks.

items.termEn: Pouring hot-brewed coffee over ice makes it taste as strong as regular hot coffee.
items.explanationShort:
When hot coffee hits ice, the ice melts immediately and dilutes the drink.
The same coffee poured over ice always tastes weaker than the same coffee served hot.
Hot cup, stronger. Iced cup, weaker.

items.termEn: A cappuccino and a latte are the same drink with a different name.
items.explanationShort:
A cappuccino is equal parts espresso, milk, and foam.
A latte, by contrast, is mostly steamed milk with very little foam on top.
Cappuccino is frothier, latte is creamier.

items.termEn: A flat white usually contains less milk than a latte.
items.explanationShort:
A flat white is served in a smaller cup, typically around 5 oz.
A latte is much larger, around 8 to 12 oz, so it has more milk.
Flat white, bold. Latte, creamy.

items.termEn: A cortado has equal parts espresso and milk.
items.explanationShort:
A cortado uses equal parts espresso and milk.
The milk softens the espresso without diluting it.
Half espresso, half milk, no foam.

items.termEn: Grind size matters more than machine brand for the taste of an espresso.
items.explanationShort:
The grind determines how much contact time water has with the coffee.
A precise grind extracts more flavor than any machine upgrade alone.
Grind first, machine second.

items.termEn: In France, a café means a small espresso.
items.explanationShort:
When you sit down at a French cafe, the default order is a tiny espresso.
It is served in a small cup, not a large mug.
No filter, no mug, just a shot.

items.termEn: A café gourmand in France is a coffee served with several small desserts.
items.explanationShort:
A café gourmand pairs one coffee with a selection of small desserts on the side.
It is often ordered at the end of a meal as a lighter alternative to a full dessert.
Coffee plus sweets on one plate, not just a cup.

items.termEn: A single espresso is typically 25–35 ml (about 0.8–1.2 oz).
items.explanationShort:
An espresso is roughly the size of a shot glass, about 25 to 35 ml.
Most people expect more liquid, but that small size is what makes it so concentrated.
Espresso is tiny by design.

items.termEn: A good starting ratio for brewed coffee is 55 g per liter, or 2 oz per 32 oz.
items.explanationShort:
55 g per liter, about 2 tablespoons per cup, is the reference point for most brewing methods.
From there, you can adjust up for a stronger cup or down for a lighter one.
Right ratio, right cup.

items.termEn: Most coffee brews best at around 200°F (93°C), give or take a few degrees.
items.explanationShort:
Water around 200°F (93°C) extracts the right balance of flavor from the grounds.
Go hotter and it scorches the coffee, go cooler and it tastes flat and sour.
Just off the boil is the sweet spot.

items.termEn: A burr grinder produces a more consistent grind than a blade grinder.
items.explanationShort:
Blade grinders chop randomly, producing a mix of fine dust and large chunks in the same batch.
Burr grinders crush at a fixed distance, so every particle comes out a similar size.
Even grind, better cup.

items.termEn: Coffee that tastes sour and bitter at the same time may have an uneven grind.
items.explanationShort:
Uneven grind means some bits over-brew (bitter) and others under-brew (sour).
A consistent grind lets every particle brew at the same rate.
Sour plus bitter together usually means the grinder.

items.termEn: Hard water can cause limescale buildup inside a coffee machine.
items.explanationShort:
Every time hard water heats up, it leaves behind mineral deposits inside the machine.
Over time, those deposits clog pipes and reduce heating efficiency.
What ruins a kettle ruins a coffee machine.

items.termEn: A weak cup is always a sign that not enough coffee was used.
items.explanationShort:
More grounds make the drink stronger, but they do not fix the real problem.
Past the right ratio, extra grounds start pulling bitter compounds instead of good flavor.
More grounds fixes nothing, right grind does.

items.termEn: Letting the kettle cool a minute before pouring makes coffee taste better than boiling water.
items.explanationShort:
Boiling water is too hot for most coffee and pulls out extra bitterness.
Letting the kettle cool for a minute brings it closer to the ideal range around 200°F.
Just off the boil, not at the boil.

items.termEn: Too-cold water makes coffee taste flat and sour, too-hot water makes it harsh and bitter.
items.explanationShort:
Both extremes damage the cup, but the problem is different each way.
The sweet spot is around 200°F (93°C), where extraction is balanced.
Too cool or too hot, both miss the mark.

items.termEn: Leaving coffee to brew longer than recommended makes it stronger and more enjoyable.
items.explanationShort:
Longer brewing extracts more from the grounds, including the flavors you do not want.
After a few extra minutes, bitterness takes over.
Longer brew, stronger but not better.

items.termEn: If coffee tastes weak, the grind is often too coarse.
items.explanationShort:
A coarser grind lets water pass too quickly, pulling less flavor.
Going finer slows the water and extracts more from the grounds.
Weak cup, try a finer grind.

items.termEn: Whole beans stay fresh longer after opening than ground coffee does.
items.explanationShort:
Every time you open the bag, coffee is exposed to oxygen.
Oxygen slowly breaks down the aromatic compounds inside the bean.
From first opening, the clock is ticking.

items.termEn: Ground coffee can keep its flavor as long as whole beans after opening the bag.
items.explanationShort:
Grinding multiplies the surface area exposed to air.
That is why aromas escape much faster from ground coffee than from whole beans.
Grind fresh, not in advance.

items.termEn: A large bag of coffee keeps its full flavor for weeks after opening.
items.explanationShort:
Once opened, the bag lets air in every time you reach for it.
After about two weeks, most coffees start tasting flatter and duller as a result.
Bulk buying saves money and costs flavor.

items.termEn: A grinder that has not been cleaned in weeks can make fresh coffee taste stale.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee oils build up inside the grinder with each use.
Over time, those oils turn rancid and contaminate the next batch of grounds.
Clean grinder, fresh taste every time.

items.termEn: Preheating a coffee cup helps preserve temperature and aroma from the first sip.
items.explanationShort:
A cold cup pulls heat from the coffee the moment it is poured.
That temperature drop mutes the aromas before you even take the first sip.
A cold cup is a flavor trap.

items.termEn: Coffee that sat 10 minutes before being served smells noticeably weaker than a fresh cup.
items.explanationShort:
The moment brewing stops, aromatic compounds start escaping into the air.
They fade fast as the coffee cools.
Aroma peaks in the first 60 seconds.

items.termEn: The first sip of an unstirred cup can taste stronger than the last sip.
items.explanationShort:
Denser flavor compounds settle toward the bottom of an unstirred cup.
Stirring once redistributes them evenly, making every sip taste consistent.
Stir once, taste the balance.

items.termEn: Letting coffee cool for a few minutes can bring out more sweetness and acidity.
items.explanationShort:
Temperature affects how taste receptors pick up different flavors.
As coffee cools, acidity and sweetness become more noticeable while bitterness softens.
Hot coffee, bitter edge. Cool coffee, more sweet.

items.termEn: A one-way valve on a coffee bag lets gas escape without letting air in.
items.explanationShort:
Freshly roasted beans release CO2 for days and need a way to let it out.
The one-way valve lets that gas escape without allowing oxygen back in.
Less oxygen inside, fresher beans longer.

items.termEn: Even well-sealed pre-ground coffee goes stale faster than whole beans.
items.explanationShort:
Grinding exposes every tiny particle to air, so aromas start escaping within minutes.
Whole beans, with far less surface area, hold their flavor much longer.
Grind fresh, taste fresh.

items.termEn: Using freshly roasted coffee always makes better coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Right after roasting, beans release CO2 for several days.
That gas pushes water away during brewing, which is why a few days of rest improves the cup.
Rest the beans, then brew.

items.termEn: Descaling and cleaning a coffee machine are the same thing.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee oils and mineral scale are two different problems inside a machine.
Cleaning removes the oils that affect taste, while descaling removes scale that harms the machine.
One cleans flavor, the other protects the machine.

items.termEn: On a home espresso machine, changing the grind changes the taste of every shot.
items.explanationShort:
The grind controls how much flavor the water picks up.
A finer grind produces a richer, more intense shot.
Change the grind, change the cup.

items.termEn: Pressing harder on espresso grounds does not fix a weak or bitter shot.
items.explanationShort:
A weak or bitter espresso usually traces back to the grind or the dose, not the pressure.
Pressing harder on the grounds does not change either of those variables.
More pressure is not the answer.

items.termEn: Cold brew usually tastes less bitter than hot-brewed coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Cold water extracts fewer of the bitter compounds that hot water pulls out.
That is why the long cold steep produces a sweeter, smoother result than hot brewing.
Cold water pulls sweetness, hot water pulls bitterness.

items.termEn: A finer grind makes coffee taste stronger and more intense than a coarser grind.
items.explanationShort:
Smaller particles slow the water down, creating more resistance during brewing.
Because water spends more time in contact with the grounds, it extracts more flavor.
Finer grind, more flavor pulled out.

items.termEn: An espresso served without any crema may indicate a problem with the machine or grind.
items.explanationShort:
Crema forms when pressurized water forces CO2 out of fresh grounds.
Stale beans, low pressure, or a grind that is too coarse all reduce crema.
No crema is a signal worth paying attention to.

items.termEn: Coffee left on a hot plate stays fresh for up to an hour.
items.explanationShort:
Heat does not stop working on coffee once brewing ends.
After 20 to 30 minutes on a hot plate, flavors go flat and bitterness increases.
Thermos or nothing, not a hot plate.

items.termEn: A machine rinsed with water after each use still builds up oils that turn rancid over time.
items.explanationShort:
Water clears visible residue but cannot dissolve the oils that cling to the machine's interior.
Those oils turn rancid over time and taint every cup that follows.
Rinse clears water, not oil.

items.termEn: Most healthy adults can safely take up to 200 mg of caffeine in one dose.
items.explanationShort:
200 mg per dose is the safety threshold for most healthy adults, roughly one double espresso.
That is a safety guideline, not a comfort guarantee.
One double espresso, roughly at the safe limit.

items.termEn: The right amount of coffee matters more than the brewing method for a strong cup.
items.explanationShort:
Strength is determined primarily by how much coffee you use relative to water.
Even the best machine cannot compensate for a dose that is too small.
Right dose first, method second.

items.termEn: Coffee can taste worse if old coffee grounds are left inside the filter.
items.explanationShort:
Old grounds leave stale, rancid oils behind in the filter after each brew.
When new water passes through, those oils dissolve directly into the next cup.
Yesterday's grounds are ruining today's cup.

items.termEn: A cappuccino usually has more foam than a latte.
items.explanationShort:
A cappuccino is built on equal thirds of espresso, steamed milk, and foam.
A latte uses much more steamed milk and barely any foam, making it creamier and milder.
Cappuccino for foam, latte for cream.

items.termEn: A thin, watery espresso is often a sign the grind is too coarse.
items.explanationShort:
Water needs enough time in contact with the grounds to pull out the full flavor.
When the shot runs too fast, most of the flavor compounds stay behind in the puck.
Rushed water skips the flavor.

items.termEn: A paper filter makes coffee taste cleaner and lighter than a metal filter.
items.explanationShort:
Paper filters absorb the oils that pass freely through a metal filter.
Without those oils, the cup tastes cleaner and the finish is lighter on the palate.
Paper catches what metal lets through.

items.termEn: Coffee stored in a sealed bag in the freezer always picks up freezer odors.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee is highly porous and absorbs moisture and odors from its surroundings.
Even a few hours in the freezer is enough for it to pick up unwanted smells.
Sealed tight or skip the freezer.

items.termEn: Freezing coffee in an airtight container can preserve it for long-term storage.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee absorbs moisture and odors through any gap in the packaging.
A truly airtight container blocks both entry points, keeping the aroma intact longer.
Seal well, open rarely.

items.termEn: Grinding coffee the night before is better than grinding right before brewing.
items.explanationShort:
Ground coffee has far more surface area exposed to air than whole beans.
Because of that, it loses most of its aroma within 15 minutes of grinding.
Night before grinding means stale grounds by morning.

items.termEn: A French press left for 8 minutes instead of 4 produces noticeably more bitterness.
items.explanationShort:
Four minutes is the standard steeping time for a French press.
Leaving it much longer keeps extracting, and the flavors that come out next are harsher.
Too long steep, more bitterness.

items.termEn: The fridge helps coffee beans stay fresh by keeping them cool and dry.
items.explanationShort:
The fridge is full of moisture and strong odors that coffee readily absorbs.
That absorption damages the flavor instead of protecting it.
The fridge steals flavor instead of saving it.

items.termEn: A flat white and a latte use the same milk-to-espresso ratio.
items.explanationShort:
Both drinks use espresso and steamed milk, but the balance is different.
A flat white uses less milk, making it more coffee-forward, while a latte is creamier and milder.
Same family, different taste.

items.termEn: Adding water to a bitter espresso removes the bitterness at the source.
items.explanationShort:
Adding water dilutes the drink, but it does not touch the source of the bitterness.
Bitterness usually comes from grind size or brew time, and water changes neither.
Dilution is not a real fix.

items.termEn: A macchiato is usually a milk-heavy drink like a latte.
items.explanationShort:
A macchiato is built on espresso as its base, with only a small mark of milk added.
That small amount of milk softens the edge slightly without changing the drink's character.
Mostly espresso, barely any milk.

items.termEn: A French press and a pour-over produce the same mouthfeel.
items.explanationShort:
A French press lets oils and fine particles pass freely into the cup, creating a heavier texture.
A paper filter traps those same elements, producing a cleaner and lighter result.
Same beans, different mouthfeel entirely.

items.termEn: A capsule machine and a traditional espresso machine always produce the same cup.
items.explanationShort:
A capsule machine manages pressure and temperature automatically, with nothing left to adjust.
A traditional machine puts grind, dose, and timing entirely in the user's hands.
Same name, different control.

items.termEn: Oat milk froths as well as cow's milk under a steam wand.
items.explanationShort:
Oat milk has less protein than cow's milk, and protein is what holds foam together.
That is why oat milk needs more time and technique to build stable microfoam.
Same drink, different technique.

items.termEn: Soy milk never curdles when added to coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Soy milk is sensitive to heat and acidity, and espresso delivers both at once.
When the two meet, the acidity destabilizes the soy protein and causes it to split.
Hot acidic espresso and soy do not always mix.

items.termEn: All plant-based milks have a neutral flavor that does not change coffee's taste.
items.explanationShort:
Each plant milk has its own flavor profile that it adds to the drink.
Coconut brings tropical notes, almond adds nuttiness, and oat contributes mild sweetness.
Plant milk choice changes what the coffee tastes like.

items.termEn: Ordering a doppio gives you roughly twice the caffeine of a single espresso.
items.explanationShort:
A doppio is two espresso shots pulled together.
Since each shot contains roughly 60 to 75 mg of caffeine, a doppio doubles that.
Two shots deliver roughly 150 mg, more than most energy drinks.

items.termEn: Plant-based milks like oat or almond always cost extra at coffee shops.
items.explanationShort:
Many coffee shops still charge extra for plant-based milks, but the practice is changing.
A growing number of shops have dropped the surcharge as demand for oat and almond milk grows.
Some shops charge extra, some do not.

items.termEn: Oat milk has been the dominant plant-based milk in US coffee shops since it launched.
items.explanationShort:
Soy milk was the default plant-based option in US coffee shops for years.
Oat milk only arrived on US shelves around 2018 but quickly overtook soy and almond in popularity.
Soy came first, oat took over.

items.termEn: Coconut milk froths just as reliably as oat milk under a steam wand.
items.explanationShort:
Coconut milk is high in fat but low in protein, and protein is what creates stable foam.
Oat milk has more body and produces a creamier microfoam under steam.
Same wand, different foam.

items.termEn: Robusta makes up more than half of the world's coffee exports.
items.explanationShort:
Arabica accounts for roughly 60 to 65 percent of global coffee exports.
Robusta makes up the remaining share, used mainly in instant coffee and commercial espresso blends.
Arabica leads, robusta follows.

items.termEn: Coffee shop prices have nothing to do with world coffee prices.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee shops source their beans through the global commodity market.
When prices rise there, the cost increase flows through roasters and eventually to the counter.
Global price, local cup.

items.termEn: Kenya is known for producing robusta coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Kenya grows almost exclusively arabica at high altitude.
That combination produces a coffee prized for bright, fruity notes of berry and citrus.
Kenyan coffee is fruity and bright, not bold.

items.termEn: Coffee ground an hour before brewing tastes just as fresh as coffee ground right before.
items.explanationShort:
Aromatic compounds start escaping the moment coffee is ground.
That is why grinding right before brewing keeps more of those aromas in the cup.
Ground fresh, more aroma. Ground ahead, less.

items.termEn: An americano contains the same caffeine as its espresso base.
items.explanationShort:
An americano is made by adding hot water to an espresso shot to lengthen the drink.
The espresso base stays the same, so the caffeine stays the same regardless of cup size.
Same espresso, more water, same caffeine.

items.termEn: Limescale in a coffee machine can only affect taste, not the machine itself.
items.explanationShort:
Heavy limescale restricts water flow and forces the heating element to work harder.
Beyond affecting taste, this kind of damage can void machine warranties.
Scale blocks water, ruins flavor, voids warranties.

items.termEn: Using a water filter on a coffee machine can help reduce limescale buildup.
items.explanationShort:
A water filter removes the minerals that deposit as limescale over time.
With less buildup, the machine needs less descaling and performs better for longer.
Filtered water, less scale.

items.termEn: Two coffees from the same farm can taste completely different depending on their processing.
items.explanationShort:
Natural processing dries the bean with its fruit still on, adding sweetness and body to the final cup.
Washed processing removes the fruit first, producing a cleaner, brighter flavor.
Same farm, different cup.

items.termEn: Most of the world's coffee is consumed where it is grown.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee is grown almost entirely in tropical countries near the equator.
Yet roughly 70 percent of global consumption happens in Europe, North America, and Asia.
Grown in one place, drunk in another.

items.termEn: Cheap espresso blends at coffee shops usually contain arabica.
items.explanationShort:
Robusta costs far less than arabica, making it the go-to choice for budget blends.
It also produces more crema, which is why it is common in commercial espresso blends.
Cheap blend, likely robusta.

items.termEn: The United States grows most of the coffee it drinks.
items.explanationShort:
Hawaii is the only US state with significant commercial coffee production.
Everything else the country drinks is imported from tropical growing regions across the world.
American coffee is mostly brewed here, not grown here.

items.termEn: Italy grows most of the coffee it drinks.
items.explanationShort:
Italy is world-famous for its espresso culture and roasting expertise.
But coffee only grows in tropical climates near the equator, far from Italy's geography.
Italian coffee culture runs on imported beans.

items.termEn: China produces most of the coffee it drinks.
items.explanationShort:
China does produce coffee, mainly in the Yunnan province.
But domestic production falls far short of the country's growing consumption, so most comes from imports.
China drinks far more coffee than it grows.

items.termEn: Brazil exports far more coffee than it consumes domestically.
items.explanationShort:
Brazil is both the world's largest coffee producer and a significant consumer at home.
Even so, it exports far more than it keeps, supplying coffee markets around the world.
Exports dominate, domestic use is smaller.

items.termEn: Most Colombian coffee bags found in US stores contain arabica.
items.explanationShort:
Colombia's high altitude and climate are ideal conditions for arabica cultivation.
That is why the country grows arabica almost exclusively.
Colombian bag usually means arabica.

items.termEn: Finland drinks more coffee per person than any major coffee-producing country.
items.explanationShort:
Nordic countries consistently top global rankings for coffee consumption per person.
Finland, for example, averages around 4 to 5 cups per person per day.
The biggest drinkers per cup live far from coffee farms.

items.termEn: Most instant coffee is made with robusta beans from countries like Vietnam.
items.explanationShort:
Vietnam is one of the world's largest robusta producers.
Because robusta is cheaper and has a stronger, more bitter flavor, it is the standard choice for instant coffee.
Vietnam beans, instant jar.

items.termEn: Ethiopia is where coffee plants originate, not where coffee was first brewed.
items.explanationShort:
Ethiopia is where wild coffee plants were first discovered, and they still grow there today.
Yemen, however, is where coffee was first cultivated and brewed as a drink.
Every cup traces back to one country.

items.termEn: Brazil is the world's top coffee grower and one of its biggest coffee-drinking nations.
items.explanationShort:
Brazil produces more coffee than any other country by volume.
Unlike most coffee-growing nations, it is also one of the biggest consumers, drinking much of what it grows.
Brazil sets the scale for world coffee.

items.termEn: Robusta coffee from Indonesia ends up in many espresso blends and instant coffees.
items.explanationShort:
Indonesia grows large quantities of robusta, which thrives at lower altitudes and in heat.
Because of its availability and low cost, Indonesian robusta is used in many commercial blends and instant coffees.
Indonesian robusta, in most blends.

items.termEn: Coffee grows in roughly the same climate zone as cocoa and bananas.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee plants need consistent warmth, humidity, and no frost to thrive.
That combination only exists in the band between the two tropics.
The best coffee grows where it is hottest.

items.termEn: Most of the world's coffee is produced in tropical countries.
items.explanationShort:
The four largest producers, Brazil, Vietnam, Colombia, and Ethiopia, all sit in or near the tropics.
Together they supply roughly two thirds of the world's coffee.
Temperate countries drink it, tropical countries grow it.

items.termEn: Fairtrade focuses on fairer trading conditions for producers.
items.explanationShort:
Fairtrade focuses on the trading relationship between buyers and farmers.
Its goal is to guarantee fairer prices and more stability for small producers.
Fairtrade changes who gets paid, not the taste.

items.termEn: A certification logo on a coffee bag always means it was grown organically.
items.explanationShort:
Different certification logos cover different things: trade terms, farming practices, or sustainability.
Only a label specifically certifying organic farming confirms that no synthetic inputs were used.
Logo on the bag does not mean organic.

items.termEn: High-altitude coffees often taste brighter and more layered than low-altitude ones.
items.explanationShort:
At high altitude, cooler temperatures slow the growth of the coffee cherry.
That slower development gives the bean more time to build density and a more complex flavor.
Grow it higher, taste it deeper.

items.termEn: Coffee prices can rise even if people do not drink more coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee prices respond to supply, not just demand.
A smaller harvest reduces available supply, and that alone is enough to push prices up.
Bad harvest, higher price, same demand.

items.termEn: Coffee can grow well in any climate if enough water is used.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee needs a specific combination of altitude, temperature, and rainfall to develop properly.
Extra water cannot compensate for the wrong climate or soil conditions.
Climate decides where coffee grows, not water.

items.termEn: A mocha at a coffee shop is espresso with chocolate syrup.
items.explanationShort:
A mocha is built like a latte, with espresso and steamed milk, plus chocolate syrup.
The name comes from the Yemeni port of Mocha, once the world's main coffee trading hub.
Espresso plus chocolate, not a special bean.

items.termEn: The Ethiopian coffee ceremony is a slow ritual that lasts over an hour.
items.explanationShort:
The Ethiopian coffee ceremony is served in three separate rounds.
Each round is brewed and served slowly, so the full ceremony naturally lasts over an hour.
Three rounds make coffee a ritual.

items.termEn: Words like fruity or floral on a coffee bag come from a shared tasting standard.
items.explanationShort:
Words like berry, chocolate, and floral on coffee bags are not chosen at random.
They trace back to a shared tasting standard used by professionals worldwide to describe aromas and flavors.
Berry in Brooklyn means berry in Berlin.

items.termEn: Coffeehouses first became popular in Europe before spreading to the Arab world.
items.explanationShort:
Coffeehouses first appeared in Yemen and spread through the Ottoman Empire in the 15th century.
They only reached Europe two centuries later, in the 17th century.
Arab world first, Europe after.

items.termEn: Turkish coffee culture is recognized on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list.
items.explanationShort:
Turkish coffee was formally inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list.
The recognition covers not just the brewing method but also the hospitality rituals and social role it plays.
UNESCO listed it as living heritage, still practiced today.

items.termEn: Coffee was first consumed as a brewed beverage in Ethiopia.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee plants originate in Ethiopia, but Ethiopians were not the first to brew it.
It was in Yemen that coffee was first cultivated and consumed as a beverage.
Plant from Ethiopia, drink from Yemen.

items.termEn: Turkish coffee is served with the grounds still in the cup, unlike espresso.
items.explanationShort:
Turkish coffee is simmered slowly in a small metal pot, then poured directly into the cup.
Because it is unfiltered, the grounds settle at the bottom of the cup.
Grounds in the cup, not filtered out.

items.termEn: The Yemeni port of Mocha gave its name only to a chocolate coffee drink.
items.explanationShort:
Mocha was a Yemeni port city and the main coffee export hub from the 16th century onward.
The name was later borrowed for the chocolate-coffee drink, but its origin is purely geographic.
Port city first, chocolate drink after.

items.termEn: Indian coffee is rarely exported outside Asia.
items.explanationShort:
India grows both arabica and robusta in its southern states of Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu.
That coffee is then exported to Europe and other international markets.
Indian coffee travels farther than most people think.

items.termEn: The chocolate in a mocha adds caffeine on top of the espresso.
items.explanationShort:
A mocha and a latte are built on the same espresso base.
The chocolate syrup a mocha adds brings sweetness and flavor, but no additional caffeine.
More chocolate, same caffeine.

items.termEn: A mocha tastes richer and sweeter than a latte because of the chocolate.
items.explanationShort:
A latte is just espresso and steamed milk, with nothing extra added.
A mocha uses the same base but adds chocolate syrup, making it sweeter and richer.
Same base, chocolate changes everything.

items.termEn: Nitro cold brew is cold brew coffee infused with nitrogen gas.
items.explanationShort:
Nitro cold brew is made by infusing cold brew with nitrogen gas under pressure.
The nitrogen creates tiny bubbles that give the drink a creamy, velvety texture without any milk or sugar.
Cold brew plus nitrogen, not cold brew plus milk.

items.termEn: Nitro cold brew always contains added sugar or cream.
items.explanationShort:
The creamy texture of nitro cold brew comes entirely from the nitrogen bubbles, not from dairy.
Most versions are served completely plain, with nothing else added.
Creamy texture, no cream added.

items.termEn: An extra espresso shot adds roughly 75 mg of caffeine to any drink.
items.explanationShort:
Each espresso shot carries roughly 60 to 75 mg of caffeine.
Adding one more shot adds that same amount directly on top of the existing total.
More shots, more caffeine.

items.termEn: Adding flavored syrup to coffee increases its caffeine content.
items.explanationShort:
Flavored syrups are made of sugar and flavor compounds, with no caffeine of their own.
All the caffeine in the drink comes from the espresso or coffee base, not the syrup.
Sweeter drink, same caffeine.

items.termEn: A bag's best-before date tells you more about freshness than the roast date.
items.explanationShort:
The roasted-on date marks the moment freshness started counting down.
Most coffee tastes best within 2 to 4 weeks of that date, well before the best-before arrives.
Roasted on beats best before.

items.termEn: Ordering half-caf at a coffee shop cuts the caffeine by roughly half.
items.explanationShort:
Half-caf is a blend of equal parts regular and decaf coffee.
The result has roughly half the caffeine of a regular cup.
Half the name, half the caffeine.

items.termEn: Adding milk to an espresso reduces its caffeine content.
items.explanationShort:
A latte and a straight espresso are both built on the same shot.
Adding milk increases the volume and changes the texture, but it does not remove any caffeine.
One shot, roughly 75 mg, latte or black.

items.termEn: Roasters who use blends are cutting corners on quality.
items.explanationShort:
Blending beans from different origins allows roasters to build a specific, consistent flavor profile.
Many highly regarded espresso roasters use blends deliberately, for their reliability and balance.
Blend is a choice, not a compromise.

items.termEn: Extra hot on a latte means the milk is steamed beyond the standard temperature.
items.explanationShort:
Standard steamed milk is warm and comfortable to hold through the cup.
Extra hot pushes it past that point, closer to scalding.
Hotter milk, same drink.

items.termEn: A capsule machine gives as much control over espresso as a manual machine.
items.explanationShort:
Capsule machines automate every variable, leaving nothing for the user to adjust.
A manual machine hands control of grind, dose, and pressure directly to the user.
Same name, less control.

items.termEn: A larger latte always contains more caffeine than a smaller latte.
items.explanationShort:
The caffeine in a latte comes entirely from the espresso shots, not from the cup size.
A large and a small latte made with the same number of shots carry exactly the same caffeine.
Cup size adds milk, not caffeine.

items.termEn: A pour-over and a drip machine both work by passing hot water through ground coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Both a pour-over and a drip machine work on the same principle: hot water flowing through grounds in a filter.
The difference is that a pour-over is controlled by hand, while a drip machine automates the process.
Same principle, different level of control.

items.termEn: A strong coffee always tastes bitter.
items.explanationShort:
Strength refers to how much dissolved coffee is in the water, not to bitterness.
A well-made strong coffee can taste bold and rich with no harshness at all.
Strong is quantity, bitter is a flavor.

items.termEn: Almond milk can separate or sink in hot coffee if not mixed properly.
items.explanationShort:
Almond milk is low in both protein and fat, which makes it less stable when it hits hot liquid.
Without careful mixing, it can separate quickly and sink to the bottom of the cup.
Almond milk needs a careful pour.

items.termEn: The United States is the world's biggest coffee-consuming country per person.
items.explanationShort:
The US is one of the world's largest coffee markets by total volume.
But per person, Nordic countries like Finland consume far more.
Big market, not the biggest per cup.

items.termEn: China is one of the fastest-growing coffee markets in the world.
items.explanationShort:
China's coffee consumption has grown by over 20 percent per year since 2010.
While tea culture still dominates, younger urban consumers are driving a rapid shift toward coffee.
Biggest tea country, fastest-growing coffee market.

items.termEn: Buying a coffee with a Fairtrade label guarantees it was grown organically.
items.explanationShort:
Fairtrade certification focuses on trading conditions like minimum prices and fair contracts, not farming methods.
A Fairtrade coffee can still be grown with conventional pesticides unless it also carries an organic label.
Fair price is not the same as organic farming.

items.termEn: The Viennese coffeehouse culture was recognized as UNESCO intangible heritage in 2011.
items.explanationShort:
Vienna's coffeehouse tradition was added to the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2011.
The listing recognized the coffeehouse as more than a place to drink coffee: a space for lingering, reading, and conversation.
Vienna coffeehouses, heritage since 2011.

items.termEn: Coffee tastes the same whether brewed with hard or soft water.
items.explanationShort:
The mineral content of water directly influences how coffee extracts and what it tastes like.
Hard water tends to make coffee flat or chalky, while very soft water often produces a sour result.
Wrong water, wrong cup, every time.

items.termEn: Mexico is one of the top coffee-producing countries in the world.
items.explanationShort:
Mexico produces arabica in its southern states like Chiapas and Oaxaca.
While it is a real producer, its output ranks well below countries like Brazil, Vietnam, or Colombia.
Mexico grows coffee, just not enough to lead.

items.termEn: How coffee is processed after harvesting can affect its flavor.
items.explanationShort:
After harvesting, the coffee cherry can be removed and dried in different ways: natural, washed, or honey.
Each method leaves a distinct flavor imprint on the bean before it even reaches the roaster.
Flavor starts on the farm, not the roaster.

items.termEn: Espresso means pressed out in Italian, not fast coffee.
items.explanationShort:
The word espresso comes from the Italian verb esprimere, meaning to press out.
It describes the act of forcing pressurized water through the grounds, not the speed of preparation.
Pressed, not rushed.

items.termEn: A daily coffee drinker needs more caffeine over time to feel the same effect.
items.explanationShort:
With regular caffeine intake, the body adapts by reducing its sensitivity to the stimulant.
After months of daily drinking, the same cup feels less and less effective.
Daily drinkers need more to feel the same.

items.termEn: Coffee dehydrates you enough to cancel out the water in the drink.
items.explanationShort:
Caffeine has a mild diuretic effect, but the water in coffee outweighs it.
That is why moderate coffee consumption still counts toward daily fluid intake.
The water in coffee still counts.

items.termEn: A bag labeled specialty coffee has been formally scored and passed a quality threshold.
items.explanationShort:
Specialty coffee is defined as beans that score 80 or above on a standardized 100-point evaluation.
That score covers flavor, aroma, balance, and defect count.
Specialty is a score, not just a style.

items.termEn: Coffee is the only source of caffeine in a typical American diet.
items.explanationShort:
Caffeine also appears in tea, chocolate, soft drinks, and certain medications.
Coffee may be the dominant source for most adults, but it is far from the only one.
Coffee leads, but others follow.

items.termEn: Cold foam is just regular steamed milk foam served cold.
items.explanationShort:
Cold foam is made by frothing cold milk without applying any heat.
Because it is cold, it sits on top of an iced drink without melting into it quickly.
No heat, different foam.

items.termEn: Coffee and alcohol are safe to mix because caffeine cancels out alcohol's effects.
items.explanationShort:
Caffeine masks the feeling of intoxication but does not reduce the alcohol in the body.
Because people feel more alert, they often underestimate how much they have drunk.
Caffeine hides the effect, not the alcohol.

items.termEn: A lungo tastes lighter and less intense than a regular espresso.
items.explanationShort:
A lungo is pulled like an espresso but with more water passing through the grounds.
That extra water dilutes the concentration, making the result lighter and less intense.
More water, longer shot, lighter result.

items.termEn: A ristretto tastes more concentrated and sweeter than a regular espresso.
items.explanationShort:
A ristretto is cut short, stopping the extraction before a standard espresso would finish.
Less water passes through, so only the most concentrated and sweet compounds are extracted.
Less water, shorter shot, stronger result.

items.termEn: A classic single espresso is usually made in about 20 to 30 seconds.
items.explanationShort:
The standard extraction window for espresso is about 20 to 30 seconds.
A shot that finishes too fast pulls too little flavor, while one that runs too slow pulls bitterness.
Espresso is fast, not instant.

items.termEn: If espresso runs much longer than 30 seconds, it can taste more bitter.
items.explanationShort:
A well-pulled espresso finishes in about 20 to 30 seconds.
When extraction runs past that, it starts pulling harsher, more bitter compounds.
Over 30 seconds and the shot turns harsh.

items.termEn: Drip coffee brewed much longer than 6 minutes can taste heavier and more bitter.
items.explanationShort:
A standard drip brew takes 4 to 6 minutes to complete.
When water stays in contact with the grounds much longer, it starts extracting harsher, less pleasant flavors.
Past 6 minutes, drip coffee starts to punish.

items.termEn: A single frost in Brazil can affect coffee prices in US shops within months.
items.explanationShort:
A single bad growing season can cut a country's coffee harvest by a third or more.
Arabica is vulnerable to frost, drought, and heavy rainfall.
One bad frost can change prices worldwide.

items.termEn: Single origin on a coffee menu means one region, not one farm.
items.explanationShort:
Single origin is a geographic label that points to a country or region, not a specific farm.
Beans from dozens of farms in the same area can all be labeled single origin.
One region, many farms.

items.termEn: Coffee was immediately accepted everywhere it spread and was never banned.
items.explanationShort:
In the 16th century, coffee was banned in several cities including Mecca and Cairo.
Authorities feared that coffeehouses were becoming centers of political debate and social unrest.
Popular enough to be banned, too popular to stay banned.

items.termEn: Coffee reached Europe through Arab world and Ottoman Empire trade routes.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee traveled from Yemen into the Ottoman Empire and then westward through Mediterranean trade routes.
Venice was one of the first European cities to receive it, in the late 16th century.
Arab world to Ottoman Empire to Europe.

items.termEn: Turkish coffee is only drunk in Turkey.
items.explanationShort:
Turkish coffee is prepared and enjoyed across the Middle East, the Balkans, and many other regions.
UNESCO recognized it as a tradition shared across multiple cultures, not exclusive to Turkey.
Same method, many countries.

items.termEn: A bad harvest in Brazil or Vietnam can push up prices at local coffee shops.
items.explanationShort:
Brazil and Vietnam together supply roughly half of the world's coffee.
When either country has a bad harvest, global supply drops and prices climb as a result.
Farm problems abroad, higher prices at home.

items.termEn: Coffee prices move only when people start drinking more coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee prices respond to supply conditions, not just changes in how much people drink.
A bad harvest reduces available stock, and that shortage alone pushes prices up.
Demand holds, supply alone moves the market.

items.termEn: Ottoman Empire coffeehouses were social spaces where people gathered to talk, share news, and debate.
items.explanationShort:
Ottoman coffeehouses brought together people from all walks of life under one roof.
They gathered there to discuss politics, listen to music, and exchange news and ideas.
The coffeehouse was the social media of its time.

items.termEn: Sufi Muslims in 15th-century Yemen drank coffee to stay awake during night prayers.
items.explanationShort:
Sufi communities in 15th-century Yemen used coffee to stay alert during long night prayers.
Pilgrims visiting Mecca encountered the practice there and carried it back to Cairo, Damascus, and Istanbul.
Yemen to Mecca to the world.

items.termEn: The paper coffee filter was invented before the modern espresso machine.
items.explanationShort:
Melitta Bentz invented and patented the paper coffee filter in Germany in 1908.
Achille Gaggia's modern espresso machine, which produced crema, did not appear until the 1940s.
Filter first, crema decades later.

items.termEn: Most coffee farmers work on plots smaller than 25 acres.
items.explanationShort:
About 80 percent of the world's coffee is grown on small family farms, most smaller than 25 acres.
For most of these families, coffee is their primary source of income.
Small plots, global scale.

items.termEn: Sufi communities in Yemen were among the first to drink coffee.
items.explanationShort:
The earliest records of coffee as a beverage point to Sufi communities in Yemen.
They valued it for keeping worshippers awake and focused during long nighttime prayers.
Coffee started as a spiritual aid, not a morning ritual.

items.termEn: The term specialty coffee was first used by Erna Knutsen in 1974.
items.explanationShort:
Erna Knutsen first used the term in a 1974 trade article.
She used it to describe high-quality beans with traceable origins, laying the foundation for the specialty coffee movement.
One woman, one term, one industry shift.

items.termEn: Peet's Coffee was founded before Starbucks.
items.explanationShort:
Alfred Peet opened his first shop in Berkeley in 1966, focused on quality beans and fresh roasting.
His philosophy directly inspired the founders of Starbucks, who came five years later.
Peet came first, Starbucks followed.

items.termEn: Starbucks was founded in New York City.
items.explanationShort:
Starbucks opened its first store in Seattle's Pike Place Market in 1971.
The founders had been inspired by Peet's Coffee in Berkeley and built on that model.
Seattle first, everywhere else after.

items.termEn: The founder of Peet's Coffee also started Starbucks.
items.explanationShort:
Alfred Peet founded Peet's Coffee in Berkeley in 1966.
Starbucks was started five years later by people Peet had mentored, but Peet was not a founder.
Peet inspired it, did not found it.

items.termEn: Starbucks had fewer than 15 stores when it started its global expansion.
items.explanationShort:
When Howard Schultz bought Starbucks in 1987, the company had just 11 stores.
Under his leadership, it expanded to over 28,000 locations in 80 countries.
11 stores in 1987, global empire after.

items.termEn: Erna Knutsen was one of the first women to work in the US specialty coffee trade.
items.explanationShort:
When Knutsen started in the coffee industry, women were largely excluded from the professional tasting rooms.
She pushed through those barriers and built her own coffee importing business.
First in the room, then her name on the door.

items.termEn: Today's coffee shop format traces back to Italian espresso bars from the 1980s.
items.explanationShort:
In 1983, Howard Schultz visited Milan and was struck by how espresso bars functioned as community hubs.
He brought that concept back to the US and built Starbucks around it.
Italian inspiration, American institution.

items.termEn: The first Starbucks store sold espresso drinks from day one.
items.explanationShort:
The original Starbucks, opened in 1971, sold roasted beans and brewing equipment.
It was only after Schultz's 1983 trip to Italy that the espresso bar model was introduced.
Beans first, lattes came later.

items.termEn: Third wave coffee shops focus on craft and bean origin.
items.explanationShort:
The third wave refers to a movement that treats coffee like wine, focused on origin, craft, and quality.
Instant coffee, by contrast, belongs to the first wave, which was about mass-market convenience.
Third wave is about craft, not convenience.

items.termEn: Erna Knutsen was born in Norway before moving to the United States.
items.explanationShort:
Erna Knutsen was born in Norway in 1921 and emigrated to the United States as a young woman.
She went on to become one of the most influential figures in American specialty coffee.
Norwegian roots, American coffee revolution.

items.termEn: Howard Schultz was the original founder of Starbucks.
items.explanationShort:
Starbucks was founded in Seattle in 1971 by three partners: Baldwin, Siegl, and Bowker.
Howard Schultz only entered the picture in 1987 when he bought the company, 16 years later.
He built it, but did not start it.

items.termEn: Peet's Coffee and Starbucks have no historical connection.
items.explanationShort:
The founders of Starbucks learned the craft of quality coffee directly from Alfred Peet.
In their early years, they even sourced their first beans from Peet's Coffee.
Two brands, one shared origin story.

items.termEn: A German woman named Melitta Bentz invented the paper coffee filter in 1908.
items.explanationShort:
In 1908, Melitta Bentz punched holes in a tin pot and used blotting paper from her son's notebook as a filter.
She patented the invention and founded the Melitta company, which still sells filters today.
One woman, one idea, cleaner coffee forever.

items.termEn: The Melitta brand is named after its founder.
items.explanationShort:
When Melitta Bentz founded her company in 1908, she named it after herself.
Over a century later, the Melitta brand is still selling paper filters worldwide.
Her name outlasted her by a century.

items.termEn: The term third wave coffee was coined by a woman named Trish Rothgeb.
items.explanationShort:
Trish Rothgeb introduced the term in a 2003 article while reflecting on coffee culture in Norway.
She used it to describe a movement that approached coffee the way wine lovers approach wine, with attention to origin and craft.
Third wave, coined by a woman.

items.termEn: The International Coffee Organization tracks global coffee production and trade data.
items.explanationShort:
The ICO is an intergovernmental body that collects and publishes data on coffee production, exports, and prices.
Governments, roasters, and traders rely on its data to understand the global market.
The global coffee numbers start with the ICO.

items.termEn: Fairtrade International certifies coffee brands for flavor quality.
items.explanationShort:
Fairtrade certification sets standards for trading conditions and minimum prices paid to farmers.
It says nothing about flavor, aroma, or how the coffee will taste in the cup.
Fair trade is about terms, not taste.

items.termEn: Most of the world's coffee is grown by smallholder family farmers.
items.explanationShort:
About 4 out of 5 cups of coffee in the world comes from small family farms.
For most of these households, coffee is the primary and sometimes only source of income.
Family farms, not factories.

items.termEn: Melitta Bentz invented the paper filter wanting a cleaner, less bitter cup.
items.explanationShort:
Melitta Bentz was frustrated by the bitter, gritty coffee produced by metal filters of the time.
She experimented with blotting paper, which trapped the grounds and produced a cleaner cup.
One woman, blotting paper, cleaner coffee.

items.termEn: The espresso machine was invented in Italy.
items.explanationShort:
The first espresso machine patent was filed by Angelo Moriondo in Italy in 1884.
Bezzera and Gaggia later refined the design into the pressurized, crema-producing machine we know today.
Italian invention, Italian tradition.

items.termEn: Achille Gaggia invented the very first espresso machine.
items.explanationShort:
The first espresso machine patent dates to 1884, decades before Gaggia entered the picture.
What Gaggia contributed in the 1940s was the high-pressure piston that produces the crema we associate with espresso today.
Moriondo first, Gaggia later and crucial.

items.termEn: No crema on an espresso usually means stale beans or a machine issue.
items.explanationShort:
Crema forms when high pressure forces CO2 out of fresh grounds into the liquid.
Stale beans produce less CO2, and low pressure cannot force it out, so either problem kills the crema.
No crema is a signal worth paying attention to.

items.termEn: The word barista is originally English.
items.explanationShort:
Barista comes from the Italian word for a person who works behind a bar.
As espresso culture spread from Italy to the rest of the world, the word came with it.
Italian word, global job.

items.termEn: The SCA Flavor Wheel was updated in 2016 with World Coffee Research.
items.explanationShort:
The original Flavor Wheel was published in 1995 as a common language for tasting.
In 2016, it was revised with World Coffee Research to add more precise descriptors.
Every tasting note traces back to this wheel.

items.termEn: Espresso culture originated in France.
items.explanationShort:
The espresso machine was invented and first patented in Italy in 1884.
France has a rich café culture, but the espresso machine and method both came from Italy.
Italy invented it, the world adopted it.

items.termEn: The Ethiopian coffee ceremony is considered a symbol of hospitality and community.
items.explanationShort:
The Ethiopian coffee ceremony is a slow, deliberate ritual performed as a gesture of hospitality.
It reinforces community bonds and is recognized by UNESCO as a living cultural practice.
Coffee as a gift, not just a drink.

items.termEn: World War II soldiers were among the first mass consumers of instant coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Instant coffee was invented around 1910 but remained a niche product for decades.
WWII military rations included it as standard issue, exposing millions of soldiers to it at once.
War made instant coffee a habit.

items.termEn: A moka pot produces espresso.
items.explanationShort:
A moka pot builds steam pressure to push water through the grounds, but far less than an espresso machine.
The result is strong and concentrated, but without the crema that defines true espresso.
Strong coffee, not espresso.

items.termEn: The word coffee is thought to derive from the Arabic word qahwa.
items.explanationShort:
The Arabic word qahwa traveled through Turkish as kahve before entering European languages.
Both the English word coffee and the French café trace back to that same Arabic root.
Arabic root, global word.

items.termEn: The Coffee Taster's Flavor Wheel was first published by the SCA in 1995.
items.explanationShort:
The Flavor Wheel was first published in 1995 to give the specialty coffee industry a shared vocabulary.
Every time a barista or a bag uses words like fruity or chocolatey, those choices trace back to this tool.
Published 1995, used worldwide today.

items.termEn: Decaffeinated coffee has been commercially available for over a century.
items.explanationShort:
The first commercial decaffeinated coffee was developed in Germany around 1903.
By the 1920s and 1930s, it was already widely available, well before World War II.
Decaf predates most people's grandparents.

items.termEn: Seeing Guatemala on a coffee bag usually signals a high-quality arabica.
items.explanationShort:
Guatemala grows arabica at high altitude across several distinct growing regions.
That combination of elevation and climate makes it one of Central America's top specialty coffee producers.
High altitude, high quality, Guatemala.

items.termEn: Climate change is not expected to impact coffee production.
items.explanationShort:
Rising temperatures and erratic rainfall are already shrinking the land suitable for coffee cultivation.
Some projections suggest that up to half of current arabica-growing areas could be lost by 2050.
Half of arabica land could be gone by 2050.

items.termEn: Hawaiian coffee ranks among the world's most expensive.
items.explanationShort:
Hawaii is the only major coffee-growing region with US labor and land costs.
That makes Kona coffee 3 to 5 times more expensive than comparable specialty coffees grown elsewhere.
Same plant, much higher price tag.

items.termEn: Costa Rica produces mainly robusta coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Costa Rica passed a law banning robusta to protect its arabica reputation.
The country is now known exclusively for clean, bright arabica grown at high altitude.
Arabica only, by law.

items.termEn: Coffee is more popular globally than 30 years ago.
items.explanationShort:
Global coffee consumption has grown steadily since the 1990s.
Much of that growth comes from emerging markets in Asia, where coffee culture is expanding rapidly.
Tea countries are becoming coffee countries.

items.termEn: Buying organic coffee guarantees farmers were paid a fair price for their beans.
items.explanationShort:
Organic certification focuses on how the coffee is grown, without synthetic pesticides or fertilizers.
It says nothing about the price the farmer received, which is what Fairtrade addresses.
Organic covers farming, fair trade covers payment.

items.termEn: Morning coffee prices are shaped by the same global markets that trade oil and gold.
items.explanationShort:
Coffee is traded on commodity exchanges, the same markets that handle oil, gold, and wheat.
A drought in Brazil or frost in Colombia can move prices on those exchanges within hours.
Global market, local cup.

items.termEn: A 12 oz drip coffee usually contains more caffeine than a single espresso shot.
items.explanationShort:
A single espresso shot carries roughly 75 mg of caffeine in just 1 oz.
A 12-ounce drip coffee, while milder per sip, typically delivers 120 to 180 mg in total.
Big cup, more caffeine, almost every time.

items.termEn: Cold brew concentrate is typically stronger and higher in caffeine than drip coffee.
items.explanationShort:
Cold brew is made by steeping coffee at a high ratio for 12 to 24 hours.
That long extraction pulls more caffeine into the liquid than a standard hot brew.
Concentrated cold brew hits harder before dilution.

items.termEn: A coffee bag's roast date tells you more about freshness than the expiration date.
items.explanationShort:
An expiration date tells you when the product is considered unsafe, not when it stopped tasting good.
The roast date tells you exactly when freshness started counting down.
Roast date is the only date that matters.

items.termEn: Milk residue left on a steam wand for a few hours can affect the flavor of the next drink.
items.explanationShort:
Milk residue dries onto the steam wand within seconds of use and can harbor bacteria.
Purging clears the inside, wiping cleans the outside.
One quick wipe now saves a stubborn clog later.

items.termEn: Ordering an extra shot on a two-shot drink adds 50 percent more caffeine.
items.explanationShort:
Most standard espresso drinks are made with two shots.
Adding one more shot to a two-shot drink increases the caffeine by 50 percent, not 100.
Extra shot means more caffeine, not necessarily twice as much.

items.termEn: Specialty coffee costs more partly because farmers get a higher price for their beans.
items.explanationShort:
Specialty roasters pay above the commodity price to source high-quality beans directly from farms.
That extra cost moves through roasting, packaging, and retail before landing in the cup price.
Quality costs more at every step.

items.termEn: Ready-to-drink canned coffee is one of the fastest-growing segments of the US market.
items.explanationShort:
Younger consumers have driven a sharp rise in demand for canned and bottled coffee.
As a result, ready-to-drink coffee now fills a growing section of grocery shelves across the US.
The coffee aisle now looks like the soda aisle.

items.termEn: An extra hot latte has a stronger espresso flavor than a regular latte.
items.explanationShort:
Ordering extra hot only changes the milk temperature, not the amount of espresso in the drink.
In fact, overheated milk can taste slightly burnt, which masks the espresso flavor instead of strengthening it.
Hotter milk, same espresso, different taste.

items.termEn: A skinny latte has less caffeine than a regular latte.
items.explanationShort:
Skinny refers to the type of milk used, low-fat or non-fat.
Because the number of shots stays the same, the caffeine content is identical to a regular latte.
Less fat, same caffeine.
